nativeRange.habitat: Warm, slow-moving, tannin-stained freshwater bodies of the Amazon basin — backwaters, oxbow lakes, blackwater streams, swamps, and seasonal flood pools.
nativeRange.biotope: Amazon blackwater / leaf-litter pool, low-flow with abundant submerged wood and detritus.

decorPreferences: broad-leaved or floating plants for cover

diet.notes: The Surinam Toad is an ambush predator that captures prey by rapidly expanding its mouth to create suction — it has neither teeth nor a tongue. Earthworms, nightcrawlers, blackworms, krill, and feeder or frozen fish are generally advised as the core diet. It will attempt to consume anything small enough to fit in its mouth, including tankmates.

avoidWith: any tankmate small enough to be ambushed

summary: A fully aquatic, flattened South American frog best known for the female's back-brooding strategy — eggs are embedded into her dorsal skin and froglets emerge directly from her back.
careNotes: The Surinam Toad is generally considered a niche specialist rather than a typical hobby amphibian. Adults are large — commonly 10–15 cm, with females reaching around 17 cm — fully aquatic, and helpless on land, so the entire enclosure must hold water with a securely fitting lid (individuals have been known to push through loose-fitting covers). They are obligate ambush predators that will consume essentially any tankmate small enough to swallow, so a species-only setup is strongly advised. Water is generally kept warm (around 23–26°C) and on the soft, slightly acidic side. Their thin, permeable skin is sensitive to chlorine, copper, and other contaminants, making dechlorinated water and gentle 25–50% water changes a routine recommendation. Filtration should provide adequate turnover without creating strong current. Sand and fine gravel are best avoided — large smooth river rocks or a bare bottom reduce the risk of substrate ingestion during feeding strikes. Trade availability is limited, and captive-bred specimens are uncommon; individuals sourced from the wild may arrive stressed or carrying parasites, so quarantine and careful handling are strongly advised.
breedingNotes: The Surinam Toad is noted for one of the most distinctive reproductive strategies among vertebrates. During amplexus (the mating embrace), the pair performs a series of underwater somersaults, and the male presses the fertilised eggs onto the female's back, where they sink into her thickened dorsal skin. The eggs are progressively enveloped by a honeycomb of skin pockets, and after roughly 3–5 months fully-formed froglets emerge directly from the mother's back. Captive breeding has been documented but is generally considered difficult. Deep water — around 1.2 m has been reported as a guideline — is typically advised to give the pair enough vertical space to complete the somersaulting amplexus sequence. Froglets tend to be cannibalistic on emergence and are generally best separated and grown out individually or in size-matched groups.

primary.name: Reptiles Magazine
primary.notes: Used as primary because the manifest-supplied Josh's Frogs root returned HTTP 403 and Josh's Frogs catalogues only Pipa parva (Sabana Suriname Toad), not Pipa pipa. Reptiles Magazine 'Caring For The Surinam Underwater Toad' is a long-form hobbyist husbandry article covering temperature, breeding behaviour, diet, and tankmate compatibility for P. pipa.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Pipidae, Anura), native range, adult size (males to ~15.4 cm, females to ~17.1 cm), habitat description, and back-brooding reproductive biology.

additional.name: Reptile Apartment
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (cited 25.5–26°C, pH ~7.0–7.2), tank size (~150 L / 40 gal for a single adult), substrate guidance (large smooth rocks; avoid sand/gravel due to impaction risk), captive lifespan (~6 years), and predatory feeding behaviour.

additional.name: Josh's Frogs (manifest-supplied primary, unreachable)
additional.notes: Manifest-supplied primary source root. Returned HTTP 403 to the fetch tool. Independent checks indicate Josh's Frogs stocks Pipa parva (Sabana Suriname Toad), a smaller congener, rather than Pipa pipa, so even a successful fetch would not have provided species-specific husbandry data. Primary role swapped to Reptiles Magazine per playbook §3 fallback rule.

amphibian.breedingNotes: The Surinam Toad incubates its eggs in honeycomb-like pockets within the female's dorsal skin, and fully-formed froglets emerge directly from her back after roughly 3–5 months. Captive breeding has been reported but is generally regarded as difficult, and is commonly associated with deep tanks — around a 1.2 m water column — that provide the vertical space the species' somersaulting amplexus requires.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Large (10–17 cm), fully aquatic ambush predator with limited captive husbandry literature and limited hobby trade availability; will consume any tankmate small enough to swallow and requires a species-only setup with carefully buffered, soft acidic water.
